blurb: The wine-red pasqueflower: silky, silver-furred buds open into glowing 2–3 in. ruby-to-magenta bells with a golden boss of stamens at winter's end, then ripen into shimmering feathery seedheads that persist for weeks.
water: Low; xeric once established — needs sharp drainage
spread: Clump-forming; non-spreading; woody taprooted crown — resents being moved
seasons: Silky furred buds and wine-red to magenta cup flowers very early spring (Mar–Apr, ahead of most perennials); silvery feathery seedheads Apr–Jun; ferny hairy foliage through summer; foliage dies back in heat
wildlife: Very early nectar and pollen for queen bumblebees and other emerging bees; silky seedheads used by birds
deer: NOT deer-resistant — deer browse it; protect where deer are a problem
toxic: ALL PARTS TOXIC — contains protoanemonin; fresh sap blisters skin and the plant causes severe mouth pain, vomiting and diarrhea if eaten. Handle with gloves; keep from children, pets and livestock.
origin: Garden selection of Pulsatilla vulgaris, native to central/northern Europe, the British Isles and southwestern Asia
habitat: Species of dry, lean chalk and limestone grassland and open meadows

shots.cap: Early spring — a richly coloured magenta-violet flower face-on with a golden stamen boss (species-representative Pulsatilla vulgaris for the red 'Rubra' selection) · © hypochaeris (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Early spring — a deep wine-red bell nodding low over the turf, the darkest tone that stands in for 'Rubra' · © onemintjulep (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Early spring — a clump of deep violet-red bells and silvery buds with golden centres · © fredur (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Early spring — a single richly hued flower over leaf litter, orange stamens at its heart · © makennahomer (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Early spring — a silky silver-haired bud tilting up on its furry stem before opening · © Axel Gosseries (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Late spring — the feathery silver plumed seedhead that follows each flower · © Shirley Laurent (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

Early spring — pasqueflowers blooming in lean, dry meadow, their natural well-drained setting · © Thierry Arbault (CC-BY) / iNaturalist

care.hardiness: Hardy in USDA zones 4a–8b, at home in Boulder's 5b–6a climate; 'Rubra' is simply a red-flowered selection of the species and shares its toughness and very early bloom.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant container-grown transplants in spring or early fall in a sunny, sharply drained spot, and choose the site carefully — pasqueflower forms a woody, taprooted crown and strongly resents being moved once established. Work coarse grit or gravel into heavy clay so water drains freely. In containers use a fast-draining alpine or cactus mix in an unglazed pot, water when the top dries, and shelter the pot over winter, since container roots take freeze-thaw harder than in-ground ones.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun gives the best flowering and the compact, tidy habit; light afternoon shade is tolerated at hot sites but reduces bloom.[1][2]
care.soil: Fast-draining, gritty soil with some organic matter; the species evolved on lean chalk and limestone grassland, so it tolerates neutral-to-alkaline ground well — a real asset in Boulder's alkaline clay as long as drainage is sharp. Never plant in wet or waterlogged soil.[1][2]
care.water: Low once established and genuinely drought-tolerant; water new plants through their first summer to anchor the root, then rely mostly on rainfall. It goes semi-dormant in midsummer heat — do not over-water in response, as a wet crown is the quickest way to lose it.[2][1]
care.spacing: Space plants 10–12 in. apart; each forms a slowly enlarging clump that does not run.[1]
care.sow: Seed of a named color selection like 'Rubra' will not come fully true, but comes largely red-toned; sow fresh seed as soon as it ripens, outdoors or in deep containers left out over winter, and seedlings appear the following spring. To keep 'Rubra' exactly true, propagate by division instead.[1]
care.stratify: If sowing stored seed indoors, give it a cold-moist stratification (damp sand, refrigerated several weeks); fall-sowing outdoors for natural chilling is easier and generally more reliable.[1]
care.propagation: To keep the red color true, divide a mature plant into 4–6 pieces in early spring — it resents disturbance and is slow to recover, but can be nursed back if cut hard after dividing. Fresh seed is easier but gives variable color; division preserves the selection.[1]
care.bloom: Very early spring — often March into April here, ahead of nearly everything else — the 2–3 in. wine-red to magenta bells opening on furry stems above golden stamens; the silvery, feathery seedheads that follow are nearly as ornamental and persist for weeks.[1][2]
care.feeding: None needed; lean soil produces the longest-lived, most floriferous plants. Avoid rich feeding, which favors soft growth.[1]
care.maintenance: Nearly hands-off. Leave the feathery seedheads — they are a key ornamental feature. Remove old dead leaves in early spring before new growth. Handle all parts with gloves (the sap is a skin irritant) and net or cage plants where deer browse.[1][2]
care.selfsow: Self-sows gently near happy plants when the ground is open and well-drained, though seedlings vary in flower color; move or thin the ferny, hairy seedlings while very young, before the taproot anchors them.[1]
care.troubles: Root and crown rot from poor drainage or over-watering is the primary killer — give it grit and never let it sit wet. Deer will browse it, so protect plants where they are a problem, and wear gloves when weeding or dividing to avoid the irritant sap.[1][2]

edible.summary: Red pasqueflower is poisonous — the fresh plant and its sap are irritant and blistering, and it must never be eaten.[2]
edible.caution: All parts of the fresh plant are toxic: Pulsatilla contains protoanemonin (released when tissue is damaged), and NC State rates the poison severity HIGH. Fresh sap causes inflammation and blistering on skin contact (contact dermatitis), and ingestion causes severe pain in the mouth, vomiting and diarrhea; it is also toxic to livestock. Drying reduces the volatile toxin, but never rely on this — wear gloves when handling, weeding or dividing, and keep the plant away from children, pets and grazing animals. Do not eat any part.[2]

gaps: Photos are species-representative Pulsatilla vulgaris — no open-licensed shots of the 'Rubra' selection exist; the deepest wine-to-magenta forms shown stand in for the red flower color.
